name: server.R
ui.R
content:
page_sidebar(
  title  = tags$h4("MATRIX DECOMPOSITIONS", class = "title"),
  theme = theme_matrix,
  fluid = TRUE,
  sidebar = sidebar(
    width = 350,
    withMathJax(),
    tags$head(
      tags$link(rel = "stylesheet", type = "text/css", href = "custom.css")
    ),
    selectInput(
      "decomposition",
      tags$strong("Decomposition"),
      choices = list(
        "SVD" = "svd",
        "Eigen"  = "eigen",
        "Spectral" = "spectral",
        "PCA" = "pca",
        "\\(QR\\)" = "qr",
        "\\(LU\\)" = "lu",
        "Cholesky" = "cholesky"
      )
    ),
    sliderInput(
      "nrows",
      label = tags$strong("Number of rows and cols"),
      min = 2,
      max = 5,
      value = 3
    ),
    selectInput(
      "matrix_kind",
      label = label_with_info(
        "Matrix structure",
        "Controls the shape of the generated matrix: dense, sparse, banded, or diagonal. All options are symmetric positive-definite."
      ),
      choices = list(
        "Dense" = "dense_spd",
        "Sparse" = "sparse_spd",
        "Banded" = "banded_spd",
        "Diagonal" = "diagonal_spd"
      )
    ),
    sliderInput(
      "coefficient_max",
      label = label_with_info(
        "Max absolute value",
        "Sets the largest absolute value used for random integer coefficients. The diagonal is adjusted automatically so the matrix stays positive-definite."
      ),
      min = 1,
      max = 6,
      value = 4,
      step = 1
    ),
    actionButton(
      "generate_matrix",
      tagList(icon("shuffle"), "Generate new matrix"),
      class = "btn-primary"
    ),
    htmlOutput("matrix_render"),
    accordion(
      open = FALSE,
      accordion_panel(
        "How it works",
        tags$small(htmltools::includeMarkdown("readme.md"))
      ),
      accordion_panel(
        "Inspiration",
        tags$p(
          "This idea came from the @kareem_carr ",
          tags$a(
            "tweet",
            href = "https://twitter.com/kareem_carr/status/1475255675718709250",
            target = "_blank"
          ),
          "."
        ),
        tags$img(
          src = "matrix.jpg",
          class = "inspiration-image"
        )
      )
    ),
    tags$small(htmltools::includeMarkdown("credits.md"))
  ),
  htmlOutput("decomposition_output")
)

name: DESCRIPTION
content:
Title: Matrix Decompositions
Description: Create matrices and compare how common decompositions reveal structure from different angles.
Categories: mathematics, linear algebra, visualization

name: credits.md
content: App made by [Joshua Kunst](https://jkunst.com) with ❤️ and ☕ using Shiny for R ✨. Code [here](https://github.com/jbkunst/visual-data-lab).

name: global.R
content:
library(shiny)
library(knitr)
library(bslib)
library(stringr)
library(htmltools)
library(markdown)

# matrix2latex funcion is needed in .Rmds
app_dir <- if (dir.exists("rmd")) {
  "."
} else if (dir.exists(file.path("matrix-decompositions", "rmd"))) {
  "matrix-decompositions"
} else {
  "."
}

app_dir <- normalizePath(app_dir, winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
source(file.path(app_dir, "helpers.R"))

theme_matrix <-  bs_theme(
  bg = "#020204",
  fg = "#92E5A1",
  primary = "#22B455"
)

thematic::thematic_shiny(
  bg = "#020204",
  fg = "#92E5A1",
  accent = "#22B455",
  font = "auto"
)

name: helpers.R
readme.md
content:
This app generates a small matrix \\(A\\) and shows how different decompositions rewrite it into simpler pieces.

Choose a decomposition, change the matrix size or structure, and generate a new matrix. The rendered result shows the factorization and the pieces used to rebuild or interpret \\(A\\).

**Matrix assumptions**:

Some decompositions do not require a square matrix. In this app, we use square real-valued matrices for simplicity.

Generated matrices are symmetric positive-definite, so the decompositions work reliably.

Random values use integers up to the selected maximum absolute value. The diagonal is adjusted automatically to keep the matrix positive-definite.

For PCA, the same matrix is interpreted as a covariance-like matrix so we can talk about variance explained by principal components.

name: rmd/cholesky.Rmd
content:
--- title: "cholesky" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(4, 12, -16, 12, 37, -43, -16, -43, 98), nrow = 3) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(4, 12, -16, 12, 37, -43, -16, -43, 98), nrow = 3)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE) ```

Cholesky decomposition

In linear algebra, the Cholesky decomposition or Cholesky factorization is a decomposition of a Hermitian, positive-definite matrix into the product of a lower triangular matrix and its conjugate transpose, which is useful for efficient numerical solutions, e.g., Monte Carlo simulations. It was discovered by André-Louis Cholesky for real matrices, and posthumously published in 1924. When it is applicable, the Cholesky decomposition is roughly twice as efficient as the LU decomposition for solving systems of linear equations. The Cholesky decomposition of a Hermitian positive-definite matrix $A$, is a decomposition of the form $${\displaystyle \mathbf {A} =\mathbf {LL} ^{*},}$$ where $L$ is a lower triangular matrix with real and positive diagonal entries, and $L^{*}$ denotes the conjugate transpose of $L$. Every Hermitian positive-definite matrix (and thus also every real-valued symmetric positive-definite matrix) has a unique Cholesky decomposition. When $A$ is a real matrix (hence symmetric positive-definite), the factorization may be written $${\displaystyle \mathbf {A} =\mathbf {LL} ^{\mathsf {T}},}$$ where $L$ is a real lower triangular matrix with positive diagonal entries. ```{r, results='asis'} cho.mat <- chol(params$mat) L <- t(cho.mat) Lt <- t(L) Lf <- round(L, 5) Ltf <- round(Lt, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Lf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Ltf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Quick check: multiplying $L$ by its transpose gives back $A$. ```{r, results='asis'} LLt <- L %*% Lt LLtf <- round(LLt, 5) Af <- round(params$mat, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ LL^{\\mathrm {T}} = ", matrix2latex(LLtf, markdown = TRUE), " = A = ", matrix2latex(Af, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cholesky_decomposition

name: rmd/eigen.Rmd
content:
--- title: "eigen" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE) ```

Eigen decomposition of a matrix

In linear algebra, eigendecomposition is the factorization of a matrix into a canonical form, whereby the matrix is represented in terms of its eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Only diagonalizable matrices can be factorized in this way. When the matrix being factorized is a normal or real symmetric matrix, the decomposition is called "spectral decomposition", derived from the spectral theorem. The decomposition has the form ${\displaystyle A=VDV^{-1}}$, where $D$ is a diagonal matrix formed from the eigenvalues of $A$, and the columns of $V$ are the corresponding eigenvectors of $A$. ```{r, results='asis'} # params$mat <- params$mat + t(params$mat) eigen.mat <- eigen(params$mat) D <- diag(eigen.mat$values) V <- eigen.mat$vectors Df <- round(D, 5) Vf <- round(V, 5) Vm1f <- round(solve(V), 5) # V %*% D %*% solve(V) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Vf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Df, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Vm1f, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Check that multiplying by $A$ has the same effect as scaling each eigenvector by its eigenvalue: ```{r, results='asis'} AV <- params$mat %*% V VD <- V %*% D AVf <- round(AV, 5) VDf <- round(VD, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ AV = ", matrix2latex(AVf, markdown = TRUE), " \\quad \\text{and} \\quad VD = ", matrix2latex(VDf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigendecomposition_of_a_matrix - https://r-snippets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/la/svd.html

}

.tabset-dropdown > .nav-tabs > li > a,
.tabset-dropdown > .nav-tabs > li > a:focus,

<!-- code folding -->




</head>

<body>


<div class="container-fluid main-container">




<div id="header">



<h1 class="title toc-ignore">svd</h1>

</div>


<h3 class="title">
Eigen decomposition of a matrix
</h3>
<p>In linear algebra, eigendecomposition is the factorization of a matrix into a canonical form, whereby the matrix is represented in terms of its eigenvalues and eigenvectors. Only diagonalizable matrices can be factorized in this way. When the matrix being factorized is a normal or real symmetric matrix, the decomposition is called “spectral decomposition”, derived from the spectral theorem.</p>
<p>Decomposition have the form <span class="math inline">\({\displaystyle A=VDV^{-1}}\)</span>, where <span class="math inline">\(D\)</span> is a diagonal matrix formed from the eigenvalues of <span class="math inline">\(A\)</span>, and the columns of <span class="math inline">\(V\)</span> are the corresponding eigenvectors of <span class="math inline">\(A\)</span>.</p>
<p><span class="math display">\[ A = \begin{pmatrix}-0.56577 & -0.90938\\-0.82456 & 0.41597\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}5.37228 & 0\\0 & -0.37228\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.56577 & -0.82456\\-0.90938 & 0.41597\end{pmatrix}\]</span></p>
<p>Sources: - <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigendecomposition_of_a_matrix" class="uri">https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigendecomposition_of_a_matrix</a> - <a href="https://r-snippets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/la/svd.html" class="uri">https://r-snippets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/la/svd.html</a></p>




</div>

name: rmd/lu.Rmd
content:
--- title: "lu" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE) ```

Lower–Upper (LU) decomposition

In numerical analysis and linear algebra, lower-upper (LU) decomposition or factorization factors a matrix as the product of a lower triangular matrix and an upper triangular matrix. The product sometimes includes a permutation matrix as well. LU decomposition can be viewed as the matrix form of Gaussian elimination. Computers usually solve square systems of linear equations using LU decomposition, and it is also a key step when inverting a matrix or computing the determinant of a matrix. The LU decomposition was introduced by the Polish mathematician Tadeusz Banachiewicz in 1938. ```{r, results='asis'} library(matrixcalc) lu.mat <- lu.decomposition(params$mat) L <- lu.mat$L U <- lu.mat$U # params$mat - (L %*% U) Lf <- round(L, 5) Uf <- round(U, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Lf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Uf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Quick check: multiplying the lower and upper factors gives back the original matrix. ```{r, results='asis'} LU <- L %*% U LUf <- round(LU, 5) Af <- round(params$mat, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ LU = ", matrix2latex(LUf, markdown = TRUE), " = A = ", matrix2latex(Af, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LU_decomposition

name: rmd/pca.Rmd
content:
--- title: "pca" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(2, 1, 1, 2), ncol = 2) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(2, 1, 1, 2), ncol = 2)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set( echo = FALSE ) ```

Principal component analysis

PCA is not another decomposition in the same sense as QR, LU, or Cholesky. It is a way to use decompositions, usually eigen decomposition or SVD, to summarize information in larger data matrices. With real data, we usually center a data matrix $X$, build a covariance matrix, and then find directions that explain the most variance. The goal is to keep a compact representation of the data: fewer dimensions, but most of the important information. In this app, the generated matrix $A$ is symmetric positive-definite, so we can read it as a covariance-like matrix and ask: which directions carry most of the information? ```{r, results='asis'} pca.mat <- eigen(params$mat, symmetric = TRUE) values <- pmax(pca.mat$values, 0) P <- pca.mat$vectors Lambda <- diag(values) Pf <- round(P, 5) Lambdaf <- round(Lambda, 5) Ptf <- round(t(P), 5) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Pf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Lambdaf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Ptf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ```

Explained variance

```{r, results='asis'} explained <- values / sum(values) cumulative <- cumsum(explained) components_to_80 <- which(cumulative >= 0.8)[1] component_word <- if (components_to_80 == 1) "component" else "components" cumulative_percent <- round(cumulative[components_to_80] * 100, 1) cat( str_c( '

', "The first ", '', components_to_80, " ", component_word, " summarize about ", '', cumulative_percent, "% of the total variance. ", "This is the main PCA idea: keep a summarized representation of the data by preserving the strongest directions.", "

" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Principal_component_analysis - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition

name: rmd/qr.Rmd
content:
--- title: "qr" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE) ```

QR decomposition

In linear algebra, a QR decomposition, also known as a QR factorization or QU factorization, is a decomposition of a matrix $A$ into a product $A = QR$ of an orthogonal matrix $Q$ and an upper triangular matrix $R$. QR decomposition is often used to solve the linear least squares problem and is the basis for a particular eigenvalue algorithm, the QR algorithm. ```{r, results='asis'} qr.mat <- qr(params$mat) Q <- qr.Q(qr.mat) R <- qr.R(qr.mat) Qf <- round(Q, 5) Rf <- round(R, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Qf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Rf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` And $Q$ is an orthogonal because $Q^{\mathrm {T} }Q=QQ^{\mathrm {T} }=I$. ```{r, results='asis'} QtQ <- t(Q) %*% Q QtQf <- round(QtQ, 5) Qtf <- round(t(Q), 5) cat( str_c( "$$ Q^{\\mathrm {T} }Q = ", matrix2latex(t(Qf), markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Qf, markdown = TRUE), " = ", matrix2latex(QtQf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/QR_decomposition

name: rmd/spectral.Rmd
content:
--- title: "spectral" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(2, 1, 1, 2), ncol = 2) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(2, 1, 1, 2), ncol = 2)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set( echo = FALSE ) ```

Spectral decomposition

The spectral decomposition is a special form of eigendecomposition for real symmetric matrices. It factors a matrix $A$ as $A = Q \Lambda Q^{\mathrm{T}}$, where $Q$ is an orthogonal matrix whose columns are eigenvectors of $A$, and $\Lambda$ is a diagonal matrix containing the corresponding eigenvalues. For symmetric positive-definite matrices, all eigenvalues are positive. This makes the decomposition especially useful for understanding how the matrix stretches space along orthogonal directions. ```{r, results='asis'} spectral.mat <- eigen(params$mat, symmetric = TRUE) Q <- spectral.mat$vectors Lambda <- diag(spectral.mat$values) Qt <- t(Q) Qf <- round(Q, 5) Lambdaf <- round(Lambda, 5) Qtf <- round(Qt, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Qf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Lambdaf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Qtf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ```

Eigenvalue sizes

```{r, results='asis'} lambda <- spectral.mat$values direction_labels <- str_c("q", seq_along(lambda)) dominant_index <- which.max(lambda) dominant_label <- direction_labels[dominant_index] dominant_value <- round(lambda[dominant_index], 3) cat( str_c( '

', "The eigenvalue profile shows how much the matrix stretches each eigenvector direction. ", "The tallest bar, ", '', dominant_label, ", is dominant because movement in that direction is amplified the most ", "(eigenvalue = ", dominant_value, ").", "

" ) ) ``` Check that the eigenvectors are orthonormal: ```{r, results='asis'} QtQ <- t(Q) %*% Q QtQf <- round(QtQ, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ Q^{\\mathrm {T}}Q = ", matrix2latex(Qtf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Qf, markdown = TRUE), " = ", matrix2latex(QtQf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spectral_theorem - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eigendecomposition_of_a_matrix

name: rmd/svd.Rmd
content:
--- title: "svd" output: html_document params: mat: !r matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2) editor_options: chunk_output_type: console --- ```{r, include=FALSE} # params <- list(mat = matrix(c(1, 2, 3, 4), ncol = 2)) library(stringr) knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE) ```

Singular value decomposition

SVD (Singular Value Decomposition) stands for splitting a matrix $A$ into a product $A=U \Sigma V^{T}$ where $U$ and $V$ are unitary matrices and $\Sigma$ is a diagonal matrix consisting of singular values on its main diagonal arranged in non-increasing order where all the singular values are non-negative. Mathematical applications of the SVD include computing the pseudoinverse, matrix approximation, and determining the rank, range, and null space of a matrix. The SVD is also extremely useful in all areas of science, engineering, and statistics, such as signal processing, least squares fitting of data, and process control. ```{r, results='asis'} svd.mat <- svd(params$mat) # svd.mat$u %*% diag(svd.mat$d) %*% t(svd.mat$v) U <- svd.mat$u S <- diag(svd.mat$d) Vt <- t(svd.mat$v) Uf <- round(U, 5) Sf <- round(S, 5) Vtf <- round(Vt, 5) # params$mat - (svd.mat$u %*% diag(svd.mat$d) %*% t(svd.mat$v)) cat( str_c( "$$ A = ", matrix2latex(Uf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Sf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Vtf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Check $UU^{\mathrm {T} }= VV^{\mathrm {T} } = I$: ```{r, results='asis'} UUt <- U %*% t(U) UUtf <- round(UUt, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ UU^{\\mathrm {T}} = ", matrix2latex(Uf, markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(t(Uf), markdown = TRUE), " = ", matrix2latex(UUtf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` ```{r, results='asis'} VVt <- t(Vt) %*% Vt VVtf <- round(VVt, 5) cat( str_c( "$$ VV^{\\mathrm {T}} = ", matrix2latex(t(Vtf), markdown = TRUE), " \\times ", matrix2latex(Vtf, markdown = TRUE), " = ", matrix2latex(VVtf, markdown = TRUE), "$$" ) ) ``` Further reading: - https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition - https://r-snippets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/la/svd.html

<body>


<div class="container-fluid main-container">




<div id="header">



<h1 class="title toc-ignore">svd</h1>

</div>


<h3 class="title">
Singular value decomposition
</h3>
<p>SVD (Singular Value Decomposition) stands for splitting a matrix <span class="math inline">\(A\)</span> into a product <span class="math inline">\(A=U \Sigma V^{T}\)</span> where <span class="math inline">\(U\)</span> and <span class="math inline">\(V\)</span> are unitary matrices and <span class="math inline">\(\Sigma\)</span> is a diagonal matrix consisting of singular values on its main diagonal arranged in non-increasing order where all the singular values are non-negative.</p>
<p>Mathematical applications of the SVD include computing the pseudoinverse, matrix approximation, and determining the rank, range, and null space of a matrix. The SVD is also extremely useful in all areas of science, engineering, and statistics, such as signal processing, least squares fitting of data, and process control.</p>
<p><span class="math display">\[ A = \begin{pmatrix}-0.57605 & -0.81742\\-0.81742 & 0.57605\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}5.46499 & 0\\0 & 0.36597\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.40455 & -0.91451\\0.91451 & -0.40455\end{pmatrix}\]</span></p>
<p>Check <span class="math inline">\(UU^{\mathrm {T} }= VV^{\mathrm {T} } = I\)</span>:</p>
<p><span class="math display">\[ UU^{\mathrm {T}} = \begin{pmatrix}-0.57605 & -0.81742\\-0.81742 & 0.57605\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.57605 & -0.81742\\-0.81742 & 0.57605\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\]</span></p>
<p><span class="math display">\[ VV^{\mathrm {T}} = \begin{pmatrix}-0.40455 & 0.91451\\-0.91451 & -0.40455\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.40455 & -0.91451\\0.91451 & -0.40455\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0\\0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\]</span></p>
<p>source: <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition" class="uri">https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition</a></p>




</div>

name: rmd/temp.html
content:
Singular value decomposition

SVD (Singular Value Decomposition) stands for splitting a matrix \(A\) into a product \(A=U \Sigma V^{T}\) where \(U\) and \(V\) are unitary matrices and \(\Sigma\) is a diagonal matrix consisting of singular values on its main diagonal arranged in non-increasing order where all the singular values are non-negative.

Mathematical applications of the SVD include computing the pseudoinverse, matrix approximation, and determining the rank, range, and null space of a matrix. The SVD is also extremely useful in all areas of science, engineering, and statistics, such as signal processing, least squares fitting of data, and process control.

\[ A = \begin{pmatrix}-0.16301 & -0.21273 & 0.96342\\-0.45732 & -0.84895 & -0.26483\\0.87423 & -0.48376 & 0.0411\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}123.47723 & 0 & 0\\0 & 15.50396 & 0\\0 & 0 & 0.0188\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.16301 & -0.45732 & 0.87423\\-0.21273 & -0.84895 & -0.48376\\0.96342 & -0.26483 & 0.0411\end{pmatrix}\]

Check \(UU^{\mathrm {T} }= VV^{\mathrm {T} } = I\):

\[ UU^{\mathrm {T}} = \begin{pmatrix}-0.16301 & -0.21273 & 0.96342\\-0.45732 & -0.84895 & -0.26483\\0.87423 & -0.48376 & 0.0411\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.16301 & -0.45732 & 0.87423\\-0.21273 & -0.84895 & -0.48376\\0.96342 & -0.26483 & 0.0411\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\0 & 1 & 0\\0 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\]

\[ VV^{\mathrm {T}} = \begin{pmatrix}-0.16301 & -0.21273 & 0.96342\\-0.45732 & -0.84895 & -0.26483\\0.87423 & -0.48376 & 0.0411\end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix}-0.16301 & -0.45732 & 0.87423\\-0.21273 & -0.84895 & -0.48376\\0.96342 & -0.26483 & 0.0411\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1 & 0 & 0\\0 & 1 & 0\\0 & 0 & 1\end{pmatrix}\]

Sources:

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition
  • https://r-snippets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/la/svd.html

name: www/readme.txt
content:
Siesta Fonts -- http://rubberducky.nu/siesta

All my fonts are unrestricted freeware for personal use. They are not allowed to be used in any sort of commercial package - if anyone's going to make money out of my creations it'll be me, thank you very much.

If you do use any of my fonts, it would be great if you could let me know - just out of courtesy - I like to know my fonts are being used. The fonts may be distributed freely as freeware, as long as they retain their readme text files. If you wish to include one or more fonts in an online archive, again let me know - and if possible a link would be great.

You may not rename or rework/edit any of my fonts without my permission, or distribute them without the relevant readme file.

-Charlotte
siesta@rubberducky.nu
